title: Cat Rice-like Segments Around Anus: Understanding Tapeworms
meta_desc: Find out why your cat has rice-like segments near their anus, why it isn't an emergency, and how a photo can help your vet with diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Finding rice-like segments on your cat indicates a tapeworm infection, which is not an emergency but requires a scheduled vet visit. Capture a photo of the segments to help your veterinarian accurately identify the parasite and prescribe the correct treatment.

What are the rice-like segments I found on my cat?

Finding small, white, rice-like segments around your cat's anus or in their bedding can be startling. These segments, called proglottids, are actually parts of a tapeworm. When a tapeworm matures inside the intestine, these segments break off and exit the body, often still wiggling, before drying out to look like hard grains of rice or sesame seeds.

Is finding tapeworm segments on my cat a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for tapeworm segments is low; this is not a medical emergency and your cat is likely not in immediate pain.
  • Schedule a visit to the veterinarian during their next available regular appointment.
  • While not life-threatening, tapeworms can cause nutritional deficiencies and irritation if left untreated for a long period.

Why should I take a photo of the segments for my veterinarian?

  • Take a clear, close-up photo of the segments to provide visual evidence, as they may dry out or disappear before your appointment.
  • A photo allows your vet to quickly identify the parasite and ensure your cat receives the specific deworming medication needed.
  • Accurate identification prevents the use of general wormers that may not be effective against tapeworms.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Tapeworm diagnosis in cats is often based on observing proglottids, which resemble rice-like segments, around the anus or in the feces. Microscopic examination of fecal samples or fecal flotation can reveal tapeworm eggs, although differentiation between Taenia spp and Echinococcus spp requires PCR methods. For treatment of Taenia spp in cats, fenbendazole and praziquantel are approved. Animals that roam freely are often reinfected through ingestion of metacestodes in carrion or prey animals, highlighting the importance of prevention in controlling tapeworm infections.

Chapter: Parasitology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 423)

faqs.question: How did my cat get tapeworms?
faqs.answer: The most common way cats contract tapeworms is by ingesting an infected flea while grooming themselves or by hunting and eating infected rodents.

faqs.question: Can I catch tapeworms from my cat?
faqs.answer: Transmission to humans is very rare, as it requires a person to accidentally ingest an infected flea. Regular flea prevention for your cat and good hand hygiene are the best ways to prevent this.

faqs.question: Do I need to bring a stool sample to the vet?
faqs.answer: While a stool sample is always helpful for a general parasite screen, tapeworm eggs don't always show up on standard fecal tests. This is why a photo or bringing the actual segments is often more useful for a tapeworm diagnosis.
